coredata.dc:title: Label-free triplex DNA-based biosensing of transcription factor using fluorescence resonance energy transfer between N-doped carbon dot and gold nanoparticle
coredata.dc:creator: Mahani, Mohamad
Taheri, Maryam
Divsar, Faten
Khakbaz, Faeze
Nomani, Alireza
Ju, Huangxian
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a new turn-on fluorescent assay was established as a platform for the sensing of transcription factor NF-kB p50 based on triplex DNA labeled with N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as donors and acceptors, respectively in the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system. The synthetized nanoparticles were studied by different characterization techniques. A labeled DNA molecule was designed to form a triplex when no target protein existence and reported its formation by the change in FRET efficiency. While the triplex DNA was formed, the fluorescence of carbon dots at 503 nm (excitation at 460 nm) was quenched by FRET between NCD and AuNP. However, presence of NF-kB p50 followed by the considerable enhancement in the fluorescence intensity caused by the release of AuNPs labeled single stranded DNA from the triplex DNA structure, used for sensitive determination of the transcription factor. This technique showed a linearity (R2 = 0.9943) in the range of 20–150 pM with a limit of detection of 9 pM for the determination of NF-kB p50. Moreover, the sequence-specific triplex-based biosensor could discriminate NF-kB p50 from the other proteins with high selectively. Our results suggest that the biosensor provides a generalizable platform for rapid detection of NF-kB p50 in synthetic medium, promising in prevention and early diagnosis of cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanobiosensor
Triple helix DNA
NF-kB p50
N-doped carbon dots
Cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DNA sequences and duplex DNA preparation
N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) synthesis and characterization
Preparation of the NCD-labeled dsDNA (NCD-dsDNA) and triplex probe
NF-kB p50 detection by the prepared triplex construct
Preparation of the nuclear extract and the transcription factor detection by the triplex construct
Preliminary feasibility assay and sensing optimization
The accuracy and specificity of the prepared triplex system in NF-kB p50 detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the working mechanism of the NF-kB p50 biosensor.

Characterization of the synthesized NCDs. (A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectra of the prepared NCD; (B) the fluorescence emission spectra recorded at different excitation wavelengths from 420 to 650 nm; (C) the HRTEM image of the NCDs; and (D) FT-IR spectra of the NCDs.

Characterization of the AuNPs and AuNP-TFO construct. (A) is a representative TEM image of the AuNPs. The inset graph is the particle size distribution of AuNPs measured by the DLS method. (B) shows the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of AuNPs (black) and AuNPs-TFO (blue). (C) is the FT-IR spectra of the AuNPs, and (D) represents the spectral overlap between the FRET donor-acceptor pair. The emission spectrum of the NCD-donor (left profile with 503 nm emission peak resulted from 460 nm excitation) and absorption spectra of AuNP-acceptor (right profile with 524 nm absorption peak). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence spectra of (a) NCD, (b) NCD-dsDNA, (c) sensing triplex probe, and (d) sensing triplex probe treated with 150 pM NF-kB p50; (B) Zeta potentials of NCD, NCD-dsDNA, and triplex probe in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4); (C) Electrophoretic stability assay of the prepared systems by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) method; lane 1: NCD, lane 2: NCD-dsDNA, lane 3: triplex probe. The concentration of DNA was 20 μM in the loaded samples. (D) The FRET efficiency in different ratio of AuNP-TFO/NCD-dsDNA.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the triplex system in the presence of increasing concentrations of NF-kB p50. Applied condition: 0.01 M of PBS (pH 7.4), 20 μL of triplex DNA solution, 30 μM of Ag+, NF-kB p50 (a to f): 0, 20, 40, 80, 120, and 150 pM, respectively. Graph (B) shows the linear correlation between the fluorescence signal and the NF-kB p50 concentration. The data are presented as the mean fluorescence intensity, and the error bars indicate the standard deviation (n = 3). (C) The specificity evaluation of the triplex system for NF-kB p50. The data are mean fluorescence intensity of three independent experiments, and the error bars represent the standard deviation of the mean. CEA: carcinoembryonic antigen; BSA: bovine serum albumin.
Sequences of three DNA strands used in the triplex structure.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The underlined sequences depict the NF-kB p50 binding regions.

head.ce:title: Label-free triplex DNA-based biosensing of transcription factor using fluorescence resonance energy transfer between N-doped carbon dot and gold nanoparticle

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Department of Chemistry, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210093, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a new turn-on fluorescent assay was established as a platform for the sensing of transcription factor NF-kB p50 based on triplex DNA labeled with N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as donors and acceptors, respectively in the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system. The synthetized nanoparticles were studied by different characterization techniques. A labeled DNA molecule was designed to form a triplex when no target protein existence and reported its formation by the change in FRET efficiency. While the triplex DNA was formed, the fluorescence of carbon dots at 503 nm (excitation at 460 nm) was quenched by FRET between NCD and AuNP. However, presence of NF-kB p50 followed by the considerable enhancement in the fluorescence intensity caused by the release of AuNPs labeled single stranded DNA from the triplex DNA structure, used for sensitive determination of the transcription factor. This technique showed a linearity (R2 = 0.9943) in the range of 20–150 pM with a limit of detection of 9 pM for the determination of NF-kB p50. Moreover, the sequence-specific triplex-based biosensor could discriminate NF-kB p50 from the other proteins with high selectively. Our results suggest that the biosensor provides a generalizable platform for rapid detection of NF-kB p50 in synthetic medium, promising in prevention and early diagnosis of cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: Transcription factors (TFs) are a group of specific DNA sequence-binding proteins contributing to the modulation of gene expression and have been regarded as promising biomarkers for diagnosing several diseases. TFs directly interpret the genome, performing the first step in decoding the DNA sequence. They play an essential role in the processes that occur within a cell, such as genome regulation, DNA replication, gene dictation, and cellular specialization [1–3].

Nuclear factor-kappa B p50 (NF-kB p50) is a ubiquitous type of TFs existing in most cells [4]. NF-kB p50 protein interacts with sequence-specific double-stranded DNA in the genome to form a complex with significant stability. Generally, it is determined that the TFs are in low abundance in the initial steps of diseases. Accordingly, analyzing the protein concentration through NF-kB/DNA binding is an important issue to study their operation. However, the total effect of a TF in the cell arises from the influence of TF on the target genes regulation. TFs can either enhance or inhibit transcriptional activity. They can activate or repress the transcription depending on the specific gene regulatory context (co-bound TFs, cell type, isoform, post-translational regulation, etc.) [5]. Because of their significant role in the gene expression modulation, the detection of TFs can provide valuable biological information [6]. They generally exist in living cells at a low level and metabolically inactive form. The regular activity of TFs is crucial for the maintenance of normal cellular functions [7]. Disrupting TFs' activity can lead to several pathological conditions, such as abnormal hormonal responses, growth disorders, cancer, inflammation, and autoimmunity. Dysregulation of TFs function is attributed to some tumorigenesis events [8]. Therefore, it is suggested that the accurate interpretation of the TFs’ level may be help diagnose various cancers and potentially other diseases, such as breast cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma, gastric cancer, and others. However, currently, there is no sensitive and robust approach to evaluate the TFs levels non-invasively [9]. Therefore, one of the most important challenges in disease diagnosis, prediction and treatment is the design of effective, sensitive and convenient methods for monitoring the activity/level of TFs [10].

The conventional approaches typically employed for the detection of TFs include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [11], electrophoretic mobility shift assays (EMSA) [9], DNA footprinting [12], western blotting [13] and affinity chromatography [14]. However, all of these methods suffer from being complex, expensive, and time-consuming and typically involve either radio-labeling or specific monoclonal antibodies against each transcription factor [10,15]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a sensitive, cost-effective, and rapid diagnostic method for TFs level detection.

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) combined with the appropriate quantitative screening platforms provides a high sensitivity of biomolecular detection, sometimes to a single-molecule level [16]. FRET is a radiationless process with the energy transfer from a fluorescent donor molecule to an acceptor molecule. FRET can be used as an efficient signal-transduction tool for TF detection [1]. For example, in a study, two short double-stranded (ds) DNA molecules, each containing half of the protein-binding site, were labeled by a donor and an acceptor fluorescence dye, respectively. The presence of protein resulted in the connection of the two dsDNA fragments, consequently led to a high FRET signal and thus created a sensitive means for TF quantification [17].

Recently, the triplex-forming oligonucleotides have been considered efficient diagnosis methods through the sequence-specific detection of the dsDNA targets [18]. The triple helix DNA is formed when three oligonucleotides hybridize with each other to procedure a three-stranded DNA structure. The triplex DNA can be formed as the intermolecular or intramolecular conformation. In the intermolecular conformation a Watson–Crick dsDNA bind to another (third) strand of DNA by forming Hoogsteen or reverse-Hoogsteen hydrogen bonds. The third strand can either be from the alternate chromosome or a triplex-forming oligonucleotide (TFO). The intramolecular triplex DNA conformation is designed by the antiparallel binding of the duplex into the major groove of the homopurine and purine strand. This later conformation is usually stabilized by the T-A∗A and C-G∗G base triplets.

In recent years, several analyses were performed based on triple helix formation for the detection of different targets. Typically, in a FRET-based assay, formation a triplex DNA structure resulted in the signal-off arises from energy transfer between two dyes as quencher and acceptor connected to the DNA strands. The quencher-labeled oligonucleotide strand released in the existence of the TF target molecule, led to the turn up of the fluorescence signal used to detect the transcription factor [19,20]. However, the traditional organic dye fluorophores are limited by their high cost and photobleaching effect, making them less favorable as a reliable and long-term detection. The quantum dots and plasmonic nanostructures have attracted great attention as the fluorescence labels in biosensing due mainly to their superior brightness, longer half-life, and better stability than the conventional fluorophores.

The N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with broad excitation and narrow emission spectra, improved photostability, low toxicity, and high quantum yield have shown to be efficient FRET acceptors when paired with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [21]. It was found that the optical characteristics of AuNPs perfectly fitts the NCDs fluorescence quenching due to the superlative overlap between the NCD emission spectrum and AuNP absorption spectrum. Meanwhile, it is assumed that the NCD and AuNP couple can create a great opportunity for a highly sensitive biomarker diagnosis through the FRET techniques [22].

In this study, we developed a novel label-free NCD-AuNPs FRET detection biosensor to detect NF-kB p50 as a model of the TFs. To the best of our knowledge it is the first triple helical DNA labeled with NCD and AuNP pair that is established for the FRET-based NF-kB p50 sensing. This NCD-AuNP FRET detection biosensor composed of the triplex DNA formed of a dsDNA and a single strand TFO and these two parts were labeled with the NCD fluorophore and AuNP quencher respectively. Here, the NCD-dsDNA possessed a stronger affinity for NF-kB p50 protein than TFO. Our data showed that the developed triplex DNA-based biosensor could precisely detect and quantify NF-kB p50 through the light-on fluorescence detection strategy.

NF-kB p50 standard was purchased from Abcam (Abcam Co, Uk). HeLa cells (human cervical epithelial carcinoma cells) were from the National Cell Bank of Pasteur Institute of Iran (NCBI). Citric acid, sodium hydroxide, and sulfuric acid were obtained from Merck (Merck Co, Darmstadt, Germany). Tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine (TCEP), EDC (1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride), NHS (N-hydroxysuccinimide), ethylenediamine (EDA), and quinine homo sulfate were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (Sigma-Aldrich Co, Germany). Tetrachloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4. 4H2O (was from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (10 mM, pH 7.4) was from Mecrk. All of the chemicals were at the analytical grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA sequences and duplex DNA preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The oligonucleotide sequences designed to form nucleic acid triplex for NF-kB p50 sensing are presented in Table 1 . All oligonucleotides were purchased from Takapuzist (Takapuzist Co, Tehran, Iran). The fidelity of the sequences was confirmed by the sequencing and gel electrophoresis analyses. The duplex DNA was prepared by hybridizing the single-stranded probe 1 and probe 2. For that, 5 μΜ of probe1 and probe 2 in DEPC-treated water was mixed and incubated at 95 °C for 5 min. Then, the mixture was slowly cooled down to room temperature over 30 min to allow the probes to appropriately fold into the final structural conformation of the designed duplex DNA. The prepared duplex DNA, then, was used for further bioconjugation and triplex analysis.

For fluorescence analysis, a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Varian Co, Australia), and for UV–Vis absorption, a Varian Cary 50 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Varian Co., Australia) was used. All Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) analyses were recorded on a Bruker TENSOR 27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Optics GmbH, Ettlingen, Germany). A high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Zeiss-EM10C-100 KV, Germany) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV was applied to characterize the morphology and particle size. For HRTEM imaging, the 200-mesh size copper grid was used, and the samples were dried under the vacuum. The zeta potential and size distribution of the samples were measured by Zetasizer NanoZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, Worcestershire, UK).

The AuNPs with an average particle size of ∼12 nm were prepared by a previously published method [23] using HAuCl4 solution and sodium citrate as the reducing agent. Briefly, an aqueous solution of trisodium citrate (1 mL, 40 mM) was flash-injected to a boiling solution of HAuCl4 (10 mL, 0.78 mM) under vigorous stirring. The mixture was vigorously stirred until the color changed from pale yellow to red. Then, the heat was removed, and the solution was allowed to moderately stir for further 20 min. Next, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, aliquoted, and stored at 4 °C until used.

The AuNP-TFO was prepared according to the method reported by Mirkin et al. [24]. Briefly, 5 μL of the thiolated single-strand TFO (5 μM) was treated with TCEP (20 mM) for 30 min, then mixed with 500 μL AuNPs, and the final solution was mixed for 24 h at room temperature. The resulting solution was centrifuged (13000 rpm, 30 min), the supernatant was discarded, and the obtained AuNP-TFO construct was redispersed in 10 mL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) synthesis and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs were prepared by a single-step hydrothermal method as described by Zhang et al. [25]. For that, the precursors, including citric acid (as a carbon source, 210 mg) and EDA (as a nitrogen dopant, 180 mg), were dissolved in 8 mL distilled water, and the mixture was stirred to prepare a transparent solution. Afterward, the solution was heated at 200 °C for 6 h in the teflon-lined autoclave. The resulting bright brown suspension was then left to cool down at ambient temperature. Next, the mixture was dialysis against deionized water using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the dialysate was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min to separate the untreated residues.

To determine the quantum yield (QY) of the NCD, a relative comparison approach was applied by using quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 0.54) as the reference control [26]. The absorbance and fluorescence emission spectra (at their optimal excitation wavelengths) of the reference and NCD solutions were recorded at different concentrations. The calculation of the relative fluorescence quantum yield of NCDs was based on the following equation:
(1) Qx = Qr [Gradx/Gradr] [ƞx/ƞr]2
where Q is the quantum yield, Grad is the slope of the linear regression of the fluorescence intensity versus optical density, and ƞ is the refractive index of the sample. The subscripts x and r refer to the NCD and the reference samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the NCD-labeled dsDNA (NCD-dsDNA) and triplex probe
ce:section.ce:para: The dsDNA structure was labeled with the NCD in the presence of EDC/NHS [27]. Briefly, 20 μL of EDC (60 mM) and 20 μL of NHS (30 mM) were added to 500 μL of carboxyl-modified NCD [25], and the solution was stirred for 30 min, at room temperature. Subsequently, 5 μL of dsDNA (5 μM) was added to the above solution and allowed to react for 6 h at room temperature while stirring. In the end, the unreacted DNA strands were separated from the mixture by centrifuging at 12000×g for 30 min.

The final FRET-based triplex probe was constructed through the self-assembly of NCD-dsDNA and AuNP-TFO. To hybridize two sections of the triplex construct, the prepared NCD-dsDNA and AuNP-TFO parts were mixed at equal molar ratios (5 μM, 550 μL) in the presence of 30 μM AgNO3. Here, the silver ions enhance the stability of the final triplex DNA construct. Next, the mixture was incubated for 30 min at 37 °C and then cooled to room temperature. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 13,000×g for 30 min, the supernatant was removed, and the sediment was redispersed in the PBS buffer as the purified triplex DNA construct.

The prepared NCD-dsDNA and the final triplex probe were evaluated by agarose gel electrophoresis assay. The agarose gel was prepared at 2% agarose concentration. The samples were loaded at approximately 20 μM DNA. The electrophoresis running buffer was 1 × Tris, borate, and EDTA (TBE), and a voltage of 100 V was applied to the samples for 40 min. The gel, then, was stained by Gel Red (Merck Co.) for 15 min and imaged under the UV light to capture the DNA and NCD band locations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NF-kB p50 detection by the prepared triplex construct
ce:section.ce:para: To detect the NF-kB p50 transcription factor, different amounts of NF-kB p50 were spiked into the triplex probe solution (5 μM, 20 μL) in 10 mM PBS and then incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Next, the solution was transferred into a quartz spectrofluorimeter cell, and the emission spectrum was recorded between 460 and 650 nm at the excitation wavelength of 460 nm. The results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three independent measurements for each concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the nuclear extract and the transcription factor detection by the triplex construct
ce:section.ce:para: To further validate our triplex construct functionality, we tested its practical application for the transcription factor detection present in the HeLa cells’ nuclear extract. Before nuclear extraction, the HeLa cells were maintained in 75 cm2 culture flasks, using RPMI 1640, supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum, and 1% penicillin-streptomycin, at 37 °C, 5% CO2 humidified incubator. The HeLa cell nuclear extracts were prepared by a nuclear extraction kit (ActiveMotif, Carlsbad, CA) according to the Nuclear Extract Manual protocol, and the concentration of proteins was measured using Bradford-based assay.

In this study, we used a triplex DNA construct to design a label-free and turn-on fluorescence biosensor. Fig. 1 shows the general scheme of the biosensor strategy and preparation. To prepare this biosensor, first, the thiolated-TFO was labeled with AuNP through the Au–S bond. Afterward, the aminolated-dsDNA sequence was labeled with the carboxyl-modified NCD using EDC/NHS via covalent amide bond formation. The dsDNA contained a recognition site for NF-kB p50 that moderately covered the homopyrimidine strand with AuNP-TFO. Next, the TFO was overlapped with NCD-dsDNA containing the recognition site via Hoogsteen bonding. This step was performed in the presence of Ag+ as the helper ion to form the final triplex DNA conformation.

In principle, the designed biosensor is based on the FRET concept occurring between NCD and AuNP through DNA hybridization. Here, NCD act as an emission donor and AuNP as a fluorescence quencher. To have the functional biosensor, the design of the triplex should result in a significantly higher affinity of NCD-dsDNA to the NF-kB p50 molecule than AuNP-TFO [28]. Therefore, NF-kB p50 can relax the triplex DNA and release the AuNP-TFO. The release of AuNP-TFO, in turn, can decrease the FRET effect, and thereby the fluorescence emission of NCD is restored.

The NCDs were hydrothermally synthesized using citric acid in the presence of EDA to perform the N-doping process and improve photoluminescence efficiency for N-type doped carbon dots. Fig. 2 A shows the UV–Vis absorption (black line) and photoluminescence (blue line) properties of the aqueous solution of the prepared NCDs. The absorption spectrum of NCD is in agreement with the literature [29]. The exhibited shoulder at 235 nm and a strong absorption peak centered at 330 nm ascribed to the π -π∗ and n-π∗ transitions respectively.

Typically, NCDs show an excitation-dependent fluorescence property when there is a wide range of particle sizes and surface states in a sample [30]. Thus, it is informative to know whether the prepared NCDs show this excitation-dependent emission feature. As shown in Fig. 2B, when the excitation wavelength increased from 420 to 500 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity changed, and a red-shift in the wavelength was observed. The maximum fluorescence intensity at 503 nm was obtained with the excitation wavelength of 460 nm. This character can be attributed to the quantum dimensions influencing the bandgap of NCD [31].
The morphology and size distribution of NCDs were evaluated by HRTEM image. As shown in Fig. 2C, the NCDs were monodisperse and spherical nanocrystals with an average size of about 8 nm.

The FT-IR analysis was used to identify the surface functional groups of the prepared NCDs and obtain more structural insight (Fig. 2D). The broad absorption peak at 3388 cm-1 was related to the stretching of O–H and N–H. The characteristic absorption peak at 2953 cm-1 represented the stretching vibrations of C–H. Moreover, the peaks at 1657 cm-1 and 1050 cm-1 were likely assigned to the stretching vibration bands of CO and C–O, respectively. Therefore, the FT-IR results indicated the presence of functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine, on the surface of the prepared NCDs.

The QY of the NCD was calculated by linear regression analysis, using quinine hemisulfate as the reference standard. Fig. S1 shows the fluorescent intensity vs. optical absorbance of quinine hemisulfate (A) and NCD (B) at various concentrations. The slope of the graph indicated that the QY of the prepared NCD was 5.6% compared to the quinine hemisulfate.

The TEM image (Fig. 3 A) indicated that the synthesized gold nanoparticles had an approximately spherical Shape with an average size of about 12 nm. The size distribution of the AuNPs measured by DLS is shown as an inset in Fig. 3A. This evaluation showed that the hydrodynamic diameter of AuNPs was between 5 and 30 nm with an average size of 12 nm, which confirmed the observed TEM results.

Fig. 3B represents the UV–Vis spectra of AuNPs. Fig. 3B(a) indicates that AuNPs have a distinct optical feature called the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) at 524 nm. Using the absorbance (molar extinction coefficient of 3.67 × 108 M − 1 cm-1 at wavelength 524 nm), the concentration of AuNPs was estimated to be 2 nM [32].

By adding the thiolated TFO to the synthesized AuNPs and incubating for 24 h at room temperature, the LSPR absorption peak of the prepared AuNPs–TFO was shifted from 524 nm to 541 nm (Fig. 3B(b)). This shift in the absorption spectra confirmed the conjugation of the added DNA to the AuNPs, resulting in a change in the refractive index of the surrounding medium in AuNPs-TFO compared to the AuNPs.

Fig. 3C presents the FTIR spectrum of AuNPs. The peak at 3437 cm-1 can be attributed to the OH groups, and the peak at 1637 cm-1 can be the vibrational absorbance of CO groups. Therefore, these peaks indicate the presence of the carboxyl group on the surface of AuNPs.

The spectral overlap between emission spectrum of the donor and absorption spectrum of the acceptor is another crucial factor of the FRET system [33]. This spectral overlap significantly affects the efficacy and sensitivity of the FRET construct. For instance, Liu et al. [34] proposed a FRET system using carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanorods (Au NRs) as energy donor/acceptor pairs. The fluorescence signal of CDs was quenched significantly by Au NRs via the FRET mechanism. The CDs-cysteamine-Au NRs assembly was further applied to efficiently determination of lead ions. Interestingly, lead ions could bind completely with cysteamine and disturbed the FRET process, which reactivated fluorescence signal of the assembly. Regard to the critical impact of this spectral overlap, we next evaluated this property in our FRET system. Fig. 3D shows that the NCD emission spectrum) black curve (has a significant overlap with the absorption spectrum of the AuNPs)red curve(. This nice overlap assured us that the combination of the NCD and AuNP is a suitable candidate for fluorescence quenching in our FRET-based biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary feasibility assay and sensing optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The possibility of the proposed method for sensing the NF-kB p50 was assessed by the fluorescence emission intensity of the NCD under diverse situations. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the NCD displayed a high fluorescence emission (curve a), and there was a slight decrease in the intensity after the addition of the dsDNA (curve b). When AuNP-TFO was introduced into the system, including NCD-dsDNA and silver ions, the fluorescence intensity is significantly reduced, indicating the formation of a large amount of DNA triple helix (curve c). However, the fluorescence emission of CDs was recovered in the presence of 150 pM NF-kB (curve d).

Furthermore, the zeta potential measurement was used to monitor the surface charge of the particles at different conditions to prove the successful synthesis of the desired structures. The zeta potential of the carboxylic acid-coated NCD was initially −3.5 mV (Fig. 5 B). However, in the presence of the dsDNA (5 μM), the binding NCDs to DNA caused a significant decrease of the zeta potential to −20.5 mV, possibly due to the negative nature of the added DNA to the constructs. Additionally, this increased negative charge can improve the colloidal stability of the constructed carbon dot system through the enhanced electrostatic repulsion of the particles. Next, the addition of AuNP-TFO further decreased the surface charge of the particles to about −30.9 mV. These data reassured us of the successful assembly of the triplex probe.

To improve the sensitivity of the prepared triplex probe, the pH of the NCD-dsDNA solution and the concentration of TFO and silver ions in the solution were optimized, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2, changing the pH from 5.5 to 8.5 could enhance the fluorescence intensity with a maximum MFI at pH 7.4. Additionally, increasing the TFO concentration by up to 5 μM significantly improved the fluorescence signals (Fig. S2) of the formed triplex in the presence of 150 pM NF-kB p50 substrate. Furthermore, Fig. S3 shows that the higher fluorescence intensity signal was achieved when the final concentration of the silver ions was 30 μM. The Ag + ions can displace the N3 proton of cytosine in Hoogsteen base-pairing to form the base triplet CG.CAg+ and thus improve the multi-stranded DNA systems [33]. Our results, once again, highlighted the role of the Ag + ions in the stability of the DNA triplex system as a multi-stranded DNA structure.

We then studied the effect of increasing the acceptor concentration (AuNP-TFO) on the FRET system efficiency at a constant donor concentration (NCD-dsDNA). In general, TFO can interact with the purine bases in the major groove of the dsDNA, forming a rigid triplex structure through Hoogsteen hydrogen bonding. In the absence of NF-kB p50, the hybridization reaction occurs between NCD-dsDNA and AuNP-TFO through the triplex probe leading to the quench of the fluorescence of NCD. It arises from the reality that the NCDs and AuNP are located in the nearby, in the distance of Förster, hence FRET phenomenon occurs.

The efficiency of FRET (E) directly depends on the NCD molar ratio. It can be defined as the number of quanta transferred to the acceptor divided by the number of quanta absorbed by the donor [34]. Equation (1). shows the calculation of the FRET efficiency:
(2) E = [1 – (F/F0)] × 100
where F and F0 are the integrated fluorescence intensities emitted by the donor in the presence and absence of the acceptor, respectively.

In our study, we found a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of NCD-dsDNA when the ratio of AuNP-TFO:NCD-dsDNA increased. For example, by increasing the ratio of AuNP-TFO:NCD-dsDNA from 0.2 to 1.6, the FRET efficiency increased from 56.9% to 73.2% (Fig. 4D). However, we expect that NF-kB p50 has a stronger affinity to NCD-dsDNA than AuNP-TFO [35] and is a solid competitor to bind to NCD-dsDNA specifically. This higher binding affinity of NF-kB p50 can release AuNP-TFO, and as a result, the fluorescent signal significantly enhances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The accuracy and specificity of the prepared triplex system in NF-kB p50 detection
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the accuracy and limit-of-detection (LOD) of the prepared triplex system for NF-kB p50, different concentrations of the NF-kB p50 were added to the triplex probe solution, and after 10 min incubation at ambient temperature, the corresponding emission spectra (450–650 nm) were recorded (λex = 460 nm). Fig. 5A shows the fluorescence intensity in response to different concentrations of NF-kB p50. The fluorescence emission indicated a strong linear correlation with the NF-kB p50 concentrations from 20 to 150 pM (R2 = 0.994) (Fig. 5B) and this linear range is comparable or even better than those achieved with other references listed in Table S1, b cause it is wide enough to enable quantification of the amount of the NF-κB transcription factor. The calculated detection limit (LOD) was 9 pM (3σ), (Which was acceptable compared to the other previously established techniques, see Table S1) and could be employed for highly sensitive detection in, and hence, might have a promising future for highly sensitive bioassays used in clinical detection. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.7% for 150 pM NF-kB p50 (n = 3).

Next, the specificity of the prepared FRET-based triplex system for NF-kB p50 was investigated by comparing the nonspecific binding of the triplex probe to some common proteins at a similar detection condition. The concentration for all used proteins was 120 pM. As shown in Fig. 5C, the fluorescence signals of the system in the presence of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and thrombin were significantly less than that of NF-kB p50.

Moreover, to further verify our method's feasibility, the triplex probe was applied to detect different concentrations of the NF-kB p50 spiked in 10 times diluted HeLa cells' nuclear extract. The concentrations of the NF-kB p50 were calculated from the prepared fluorescence intensity vs. concentration standard curve. As depicted in Table S2, the observed results showed a significant correlation with the actual added values with the recovery range of 99–103% and the RSD of 3.0–4.7%. These findings indicate that the prepared FRET-based triplex strategy is a promising and accurate detection approach for TF in the biological samples.

In this study, based on a self-assembly triplex DNA molecular switch, a simple, enzyme-free and sensitive new fluorescent strategy for detection of intracellular NF-kB p50 was developed, achieving high sensitivity towards purified targets and real biological samples. The “turn on” detection mechanism was based on decreasing FRET efficiency between NCDs and AuNPs. Our data confirmed the efficient binding of NF-kB p50 as the target molecule to the FRET-based detection system with the LOD of 9 pM that is an acceptable concentration compared to the other currently used methods. Our developed protocol uses a safe and optically stable quantum dot technology and is a straightforward, efficient, fast, and sensitive assay to detect TF in various complex samples. The results of this study suggest the prepared technology as a promising and practical biosensor candidate in the clinical diagnosis of various similar biomarkers.

Mohamad Mahani: Supervision, Project administration, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Maryam Taheri: Investigation, Methodology. Faten Divsar: Writing – original draft. Faeze Khakbaz: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Alireza Nomani: Writing – review & editing. Huangxian Ju: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338919.
